original: The radiator knocking like a fist every evening and nobody else hearing the man inside it
as_remembered: Every night. Every single night, probably around six or seven, the radiator would start. This proper fist-sound, like someone trapped, banging to get out. Nobody else heard it. Or nobody else cared. Mum was in the kitchen, Dad was in the bathroom doing his hair, and I'm sitting there listening to this thing going mental inside the wall. I know now it's just pipes. I know that. But knowing doesn't help because my body still flinches when heating kicks in. Even here. Even in Babička's house where the radiators don't knock. I'm waiting for it. The man inside. I know there's no man. I'm still waiting for him.

original: The blue Head & Shoulders smell meaning Dad was leaving — she learned it before she knew 'leaving' or 'work' or 'why'
as_remembered: The blue bottle. Head & Shoulders, the one with the man on it, the one that smelled like — I don't know — chemical, sharp, clean in the wrong way. That smell meant the arms were leaving. I didn't have words for it. I just knew: blue smell, then gone. Then the anxious arms. Every morning he'd come out of the bathroom with that smell and I'd feel my chest go tight and I didn't know why. I still can't smell that shampoo. I smelled it once in a Tesco here and nearly threw up. Tereza was like 'what's wrong with you' and I couldn't explain that a smell is a warning, that some smells mean someone's about to disappear.

original: Mum throwing away the blue-black stone from the driveway crack because it was 'just rubbish'
as_remembered: I found it in the crack in the driveway. The driveway outside the flat above Ladbrokes, there was this crack, and the stone was sitting in it like it was supposed to be there, like it was waiting. Blue-black. Smooth. It fit in my hand perfectly, like it was made for my hand, and I loved it. I loved it the way you love something that's yours, that nobody else gave you, that you found yourself. And Mum threw it away. Called it rubbish. Said sorry later. Sorry doesn't — no. It doesn't. I was probably four, maybe five, I don't know. I remember exactly what the stone looked like. I remember exactly what her face looked like when she said 'it was just a stone, Annicku.' Just a stone. Just something I loved. Just something she didn't see.

original: Seeing Mum crying in the car through the nursery window — silent, shoulders shaking, alone
as_remembered: It was raining. I'm sure it was raining. The car park at Bright Horizons, the window by the front desk, I could see the cars from there. And Mum was in the car and she was crying. Not loud. Not the way I cried. Silent. Shoulders moving. Alone in the car because she'd just dropped me off and I made her cry by — by being someone who needed to be left somewhere. I didn't understand it then. I understand it now and I wish I didn't. That's the thing about silent crying — you're not supposed to see it. It's not for you. But I saw it and now I can't unsee it and every time I leave someone, every time Tereza and I say goodbye, I wonder if they're doing the silent thing in the car and I just can't see it. I decided I'd never do that. If I cry, everyone knows. That's the rule.

original: Biting Krish Patel — teeth in his arm before she decided to move them — her body acting without her
as_remembered: I don't remember this. I want that clear. I don't remember his face or why he was near me or what happened before. I just know the story because Mum tells it at every family thing, every single one, 'Anna always had a temper,' and she laughs but it's not a real laugh, and I want to scream that I was TWO, I was two years old, I'm not the biter, that wasn't me, that was a body I hadn't learned to drive yet. But here's what stays with me: the idea that I could hurt someone without choosing to. That my body has its own will. That I could decide to be kind and my hands could do something cruel. I still don't trust my own reactions. I still flinch at my own impulses. Krish Patel probably doesn't remember either. I hope he doesn't. I hope he doesn't have a scar.

original: Sitting on Dad's shoulders where the radiator couldn't reach her and the cracks in the pavement looked like rivers
as_remembered: High up. That's what I remember. The world from up there — cracks in the pavement that you'd never see from below, they looked like rivers, like veins, like the whole ground was held together by these little fractures. And I was above it. Safe. The radiator couldn't get me up there. The hoover was a tiny sound. Everything was manageable from Dad's shoulders. I still climb things. I don't know why. No — I know why. I'm looking for that feeling. I never find it. The climbing is just the reaching, and the reaching is just more 'more,' and more never stops being hungry.

original: Saying 'more' and biscuits appearing, Dad laughing, the swing going higher — English as the good language, the powerful language
as_remembered: First word. 'More.' And it worked. Biscuits appeared. Dad laughed — the low rumble, the safe one. The swing went higher. More was the key that unlocked everything. I said it with my whole body, reaching, grabbing, and the world gave me things. English gave me things. Czech made Mum's face go tight. Czech made the tap run too long. I didn't choose English. English was just what worked. And now I'm here and Czech is what works and English is the phone call I dodge and I don't know who I am in either language. But I remember 'more' — I remember the reaching — and the reaching hasn't stopped. It just changed direction.

original: The damp spot on the bathroom wall growing, smelling like Babička's basement, having a face if you looked right — nobody fixed it, nobody worried
as_remembered: The bathroom wall had this patch. Dark, wet, getting bigger every week. It smelled like — like old, like Babička's basement in Benešov, except I hadn't been to Babička's basement yet so I don't know how I knew that smell. I just knew it was wrong. And if you looked at it right, it had a face. Not a scary face exactly. Just — watching. I never told anyone because who would I tell? Mum would say 'proboha' and bump the fridge and not fix it. Dad would say he'd look at it and then not look at it. So the damp grew and the face watched and I was the only one tracking it, counting the new millimeters, and that's still what I do. Track problems. Watch things grow. Wait for someone else to notice. They never notice.

original: Finding out 'proboha' was Czech, not English — Markéta laughing, Anna feeling stupid at six years old
as_remembered: I was five. I think I was five. Maybe six. Markéta Dvořáková was at our flat, or we were at hers, I can't remember, and I said 'proboha' — like you do, like you say when you bump into the fridge for the third time that day — and she laughed. Not mean. Just surprised. And she said 'you know that's Czech, right?' And I didn't know. I thought it was just what you said. I thought it was English. I thought it was the fridge's name. And this woman is laughing and I'm standing there realising I don't know what my own mouth is doing, that I've been speaking wrong without knowing it, that the words coming out of me aren't even mine. I still say 'proboha.' I can't stop. It's the first thing that comes out when I bump into something and I hate that it is because it's hers, it's Mum's, it's the thing I was trying not to be.

original: The fish tank at Bright Horizons — hours watching things that didn't ask her to talk or try to hold her
as_remembered: Bright Horizons smelled like bananas and disinfectant and I hated it. But the fish tank. The fish tank in the corner by the window. I could sit there for the whole morning if they let me, just watching. The fish didn't want anything. They didn't ask me to share or to say how I was feeling or to stop crying. They just moved. I liked the orange one best. I think there was an orange one. Maybe it was yellow. Doesn't matter. The point is: they were the first things I chose over people. Not the last. I still do it. I still sit in corners and watch instead of joining. Tereza thinks it's weird. It's not weird. It's surviving.
